words: CLARICE SAMPSON: My father was George H. Moody, was born in St. John, New Bruns
wick. He was the thirteenth child and all the rest of the family had been
born in Scotland. And his own mother died when he was eighteen months
old, and his father remarried, because, I presume to take care of a
big family. My father, although he was so young, he didn't accept it,
and the stepmother allowed the older children to take advantage of
the younger ones. So, when he was thirteen he ran away from home and
was on his own from then on. He made his way to Boston and worked in
a book bindery there as a young man. His brother, one of his brothers
was a worker in marble in Vermont, and he finally moved to Missouri
and there he opened a monument business. It was the day of big monu
ments, and tombstones, they called them.
My father apprenticed him
self to his brother and learned the trade, and when he was a young
man he established his own monument works in Fort Scott, Kansas. But
before he established his own business he was in St. Louis, and I imagine
he was working in some such a concern. He boarded with a Mrs. Man-
ning, who had been recently widowed. Her young daughter, Mary Ida,
helped her mother and served the boarders at the table and she and
my father fell in love and they were married in St. Louis, Missouri.
He took her to Fort Scott, Kansas, where several of my brothers and
sisters were born. There was.great desire in many young people or
that time to go West, so he thought he would sell his business and
go out to California, leaving his family there til he was settled.
He met a man on the train who told him all about Ogden, Utah, and what
a boom town it was, so he decided to get off of the train there at
Ogden and see what it was like. Well, he opened up a small hardware
store, and finally, in a few months sent for my mother with five child-
ren, the youngest a three months old baby, and they all had their
little piece of luggage to carry and somebody had to carry the bag
for the baby, and somebody had to carry the bird in the bird cage.
And they made their home in Ogden, Utah for several years. And, I
think my father was not very happy being out of his own field, so
my mother and the family went over to Salubria, Idaho where my moth
er mother had come. She had remarried and had come there, and they
were mining there. My father, evidently sold out his hardware bus
iness and followed them. Early in 1892 he had heard about the town
up in northern Idaho, Moscow, where a university had been recently
established and he thought that would be a good place to move his fam
ily so that they would have the advantage of a good education for each
of them. So he came on alone to Moscow in, as I said, early in 1892
and opened a business; got in a stock of monuments in a little build
ing that was then on Third Street, and my mother followed. My father
found a house they could rent, and they settled themselves in Moscow,
Idaho. They lived in a couple of houses, I think, and my mother told
about taking care of an old blind lady, and I think the pay for her
care was probably a pretty good part of their income at that time.
Then my father had the chance to buy a house on Lynn Avenue, they
called it then. I even remember the numbers, 722 Lynn Avenue. And
he bought that place; it was a five room house with, I imagine it was
about a half an acreogoining it that was the garden spot. It had fruit
trees planted in it and a garden space. There in that house I was born
in January of 1894.
But, the year before, 1893, had been the bad year
in Latah County. It had rained and rained and the farmers were unable
to harvest their grain, so the only monuments my father could sell,was
he would get on horseback and take a kit of designs and go about the
country and if he could sell a monument, he would come back home and
engrave the monument in the kitchen, because he'd had to give up his
building downtown, and he kept his store of his stock of marble in
the barn. But he used to tell us about— they were just about down
to the last bit of money they had and my mother needed a sack of flour
so he went to town and purchased the flour and he had twenty-five
cents, two bits, they called it left. So, he bought himself a twobit
cigar and smoked it. But by and by things bettered themselves in Latah
County and once more he opened his shop down on Main Street this time.
I remember when I was a little girl he ordered the Ole Hagburg monu
ment, which stands on the campus. There were two boys from the Univ
ersity of Idaho who were killed in the Philippines in the Spanish—American
War. I don't know how that monument was purchased, Ait was
by public subscription, I presume it was, and the help of the University.
When it came, of course, we all wanted to go down and see it.
Well, it was so long, and such a long, heavy box, that it just about
filled up his showroom. And, I thought that was just about the big
gest monument that had ever been made. It stands on the University
of Idaho campus now.
LAURA SCHRAGER: He had the marble shipped in?

speaker: CS
words: : Yeah— no,the form was made, he didn't chisel out the form, but he

speaker: CS
words: did all the lettering on it, and it stands there on the campus yet,

speaker: CS
words: facing the northeast, or the mountains. And, to me, it was the most

speaker: CS
words: wonderful thing.

speaker: CS
words: Then, when I was still a little girl, quite young, the Administra

speaker: CS
words: tion Building of the University of Idaho burned. Everybody in town

speaker: CS
words: could see the blaze in the sky and everybody went to the fire, and I

speaker: CS
words: was a little girl and my mother held my hand and so I cried because

speaker: CS
words: she cried because the Administration Building was burning. The thing

speaker: CS
words: I hated to have burn up was -in the entry hall of the Administration

speaker: CS
words: Building, there were two glass cases, and in those cases were little

speaker: CS
words: artifacts that the boys who had been in the battalion over there had

speaker: CS
words: brought home with them. And I remember a little grass covered hut

speaker: CS
words: that I thought was so adorable, and I regreted, of course, the loss

speaker: CS
words: of those things. But the records, fortunately, were saved. The Dean

speaker: CS
words: of the University saved the records. I had a sister that year, my

speaker: CS
words: sister, Ethel, who was a senior in college. The women's gymnasium

speaker: CS
words: was built at that time and Ridenbaugh Hall, they were existing build

speaker: CS
words: ings, so the classes were held in those two buildings, but the sen

speaker: CS
words: iors had to go across town to the Methodist Church and the City Lib

speaker: CS
words: rary for classes. And, that was a great experience because, even though

speaker: CS
words: it was hardship for the students, they all rallied around and in a

speaker: CS
words: few years we had a new Administration building. But to make use of

speaker: CS
words: the bricks that were left from after the fire, the University hired

speaker: CS
words: small boys to clean the bricks. To clean the mortar from the bricks,

speaker: CS
words: and my husband, Harry Sampson was one of the small boys hired to help

speaker: CS
words: clean bricks, and a

speaker: LS
words: o my brother, Maurice Moody, five years older than

speaker: LS
words: worked on that, too, cleaning bricks at the University. Course

speaker: LS
words: that was quite a windfall for small boys. But the beautiful old steps

speaker: LS
words: the granite steps, they were in the front of the Administration, and

speaker: LS
words: then there was a wing on either side, then there were huge steps going

speaker: LS
words: up to each wing, so there were actually three main entrances to the

speaker: LS
words: Administration Building. Well, they thought they were of no value, so

speaker: LS
words: they were hauled away and they were buried where leveling was done on

speaker: LS
words: city lots. I can't remember the year, but the University garden was

speaker: LS
words: up behind where the old Administration Building had been, and some

speaker: LS
words: class reunion decided they'd restore the old steps. And so, they

speaker: LS
words: found enough of the old steps to build a flight of steps as they were

speaker: LS
words: originally up to the garden. And, of course, they're there now, the

speaker: LS
words: old steps.

speaker: LS
words: Can I ask you about your father's business trip? Where did he get his

speaker: LS
words: rock from?

speaker: LS
words: Oh, well, of course, around here there was no marble, and that was his*

speaker: LS
words: material he liked working in best, was marble. But, of course, marble

speaker: LS
words: isn t too good^outside, the elements are too hard on.. outside. So

speaker: LS
words: he had to go into selling granite monuments, too. And he got Rock of

speaker: LS
words: Ages marble and granite from Vermont, and that was shipped sometimes

speaker: LS
words: by boat and sometimes by train to San Francisco, and then put on trains

speaker: LS
words: and shipped to Moscow, Idaho. So the cost of transporting material

speaker: LS
words: to his business was very expensive. I often wonder how he did most

speaker: LS
words: of the lettering himself, until the business became so big that he had

speaker: LS
words: to employ men to come in to assist him. 4Then he had agents who went

speaker: LS
words: about, oh, all over Nez Perce prairie and all around Moscow, and as

speaker: LS
words: far as Yakima in Washington selling for him. And they would take the

speaker: LS
words: orders and they would carry the kits of designs, just about like

speaker: LS
words: those pictures, and people would choose what they wanted, or they could

speaker: LS
words: come in to Moscow and see the stock on the floor. But when my father

speaker: LS
words: showed stock it was always with such love, and when he was showing a

speaker: LS
words: special monument, without thinking, his white handkerchief would come

speaker: LS
words: out of his pocket and he would wipe it off a bit, and that showed the

speaker: LS
words: beauty that he could see in that particular piece of stone. As a young

speaker: LS
words: man he made, oh, things for our home— that oval picture frame, here

speaker: LS
words: hanging on the wall, with my mother's and father's picture in it, he

speaker: LS
words: made that out of marble. And how he ever could chisel that out and get

speaker: LS
words: it so perfect, I don't understand. And on the table there is a little

speaker: LS
words: marble book with my mother's initia

speaker: LS
words: engraved on and it was to represent a little white bible. He gave it to her as

speaker: LS
words: an engagement pre

speaker: LS
words: sent. So I treasure my things that I have that he has made, very much.

speaker: LS
words: As that old book over there on the table, he made.. But later,

speaker: LS
words: then, he got— he would ship in some marble for interior use, like in

speaker: LS
words: bank buildings and so on, from Italy. And then, he got beautiful green granite from

speaker: LS
words: Alaska, and then from Georgia, some

speaker: LS
words: very beautiful stone came. But monuments in those days were inclined

speaker: LS
words: to be big, if possible, if the family finances warranted that, and

speaker: LS
words: they would sit on base the same as the monument itself was made

speaker: LS
words: of, and that in turn would sit on a rough granite base. And those

speaker: LS
words: rough granite bases came from over here in Washington. I can't just

speaker: LS
words: remember where. But that was the style in those days of the monu

speaker: LS
words: ment business, and they would have individual stones that were large.

speaker: LS
words: And, then, always at the foot, would be a footstone; smaller with the

speaker: LS
words: initial of the person who was buried there on the footstone. Often

speaker: LS
words: friends would ask my father to put his name someplace down on one side

speaker: LS
words: showing that he had engraved that particular stone. I see many monu

speaker: LS
words: ments in the cemetery with either GHM or Moody down in one little cor

speaker: LS
words: ner at the back of the monument, showing that he had engraved that monu

speaker: LS
words: ment.

speaker: LS
words: Did he go through periods that were harder times?

speaker: CS
words: Well, after that year his business just gradually grew and grew, and

speaker: CS
words: he kept having more agents out and selling more monuments. And you'd

speaker: CS
words: be surprised to know that the Indians were some of the very best buyers

speaker: CS
words: of monuments. That just appealed to them. When they'd come into his

speaker: CS
words: shop, he had rather a quiet voice, and Indians liked that, they don't

speaker: CS
words: like to be spoken— often, we think we need to speak in louder tones

speaker: CS
words: to someone who is different than we are, you know, for fear they won't

speaker: CS
words: understand. But, they liked him. I know he had a namesake, an Indian

speaker: CS
words: baby named for him down on the prairie: George Moody. Afterwards I

speaker: CS
words: knew that baby, when he grew to be a man.

speaker: LS
words: Were there any epidemics?

speaker: CS
words: I remember when I was a little girl, there were time we had a smallpox epidemic

speaker: CS
words: and we all had to be vaccinated, of

speaker: CS
words: course. I was taken to Dr. Watkins' office and had my arm scraped

speaker: CS
words: with this— they used a quill, in those days, and would scrape the

speaker: CS
words: skin on your arm and put the vaccine on it. I remember, I cried and

speaker: CS
words: another little girl laughed at me, and I never liked that little girl

speaker: CS
words: because she laughed. . I thought it hurt.

speaker: LS
words: Was this Dr. Watkins?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. That was Carol Brink's grandfather. And then, my brother

speaker: CS
words: who was five years older than I had diphtheria at one time. I was

speaker: CS
words: quite small at the time, and it seemed from what my mother told me, I

speaker: CS
words: played right around in the same room where he was and I didn't take it.

speaker: CS
words: So, I don t think there was an epidemic or anything. I think. just

speaker: CS
words: happened to be one case. And, then, of course, they quarantined the

speaker: CS
words: homes in those days when people had scarlet fever or diphtheria or

speaker: CS
words: smallpox. And there would be a sign put up by the front door—Quar

speaker: CS
words: antined— and the name of the disease. And us children, of course, if

speaker: CS
words: we'd ever see that on somebody's front door, we'd cover our mouths

speaker: CS
words: with our handkerchiefs and just run by as fast as we could, so we

speaker: CS
words: wouldn't get that germ.

speaker: LS
words: Could you describe some of the stores in Moscow?

speaker: CS
words: Well, Moscow was always quite a good trading center, even in the early

speaker: CS
words: days. Of course, the first store, I think, was the McConnell-McGuire

speaker: CS
words: Store, and that was where the— Mr. Brown's furniture store is now,

speaker: CS
words: the Building, now we call it. And, later Mr. F. A. David was

speaker: CS
words: associated with that firm, and then that store went broke and it was

speaker: CS
words: started again, and I don't know the year, but it was started again by

speaker: CS
words: Motter and Wheeler. This advertisement doesn't give— but I can

speaker: CS
words: remember this store— yes, Motter-Wheeler Company, The Hustlers, and

speaker: CS
words: they had a fall opening, and it doesn't say what year it was. But

speaker: CS
words: some of the prices are very interesting: ladies' wrappers, the seven

speaker: CS
words: ty five cent ones, went at fifty cents. And the mens' hat- a seventy

speaker: CS
words: five cent hat went at sixty cents. And there were - all during that

speaker: CS
words: opening they had specials of fruit jars and Golden Crown tomato catsup,

speaker: CS
words: regular fifty cent special at twenty cents or three for fifty cents.

speaker: LS
words: This happened before your time?

speaker: CS
words: I remember this store, because, you see the McConnel-McGuire store

speaker: CS
words: had gone broke, and I don't remember when that was there, but I re

speaker: CS
words: member this store, this particular store. But I don't remember of

speaker: CS
words: the Moody family trading there very much. We were customers of

speaker: CS
words: Creighton's store, in regard to dry goods and clothing, and then we

speaker: CS
words: bought out groceries from David's store. Mr. David had started up

speaker: CS
words: his store. They sent a man around each morning to your kitchen door

speaker: CS
words: to take orders for groceries that you might need, and then they del

speaker: CS
words: ivered them with a horse and cart later in the day, which was a great

speaker: CS
words: convenience in those days, because in the winter the snow was quite

speaker: CS
words: deep, and in the spring and fall the mud was deep and it was a great

speaker: CS
words: convenience to have things brought to your door. Creighton's store

speaker: CS
words: had a delivery boy, and my brother, Maurice, was their delivery boy

speaker: CS
words: at one time. That was after the bicycles came in- we had bicycles.

speaker: CS
words: There were several dressmakers in town and if they'd call and want a

speaker: CS
words: pattern, a certain pattern, or thread or something, their delivery

speaker: CS
words: boy would get on the bicycle and go and take it to the dressmaker.

speaker: CS
words: That was a great convenience, of course. I remember one time, being

speaker: CS
words: the youngest in the family, a great many of my clothes were made over

speaker: CS
words: but I never seemed to mind, because, they were of much nicer material

speaker: CS
words: than would hrfWtbeen purchased probably for a little girl. But, I re

speaker: CS
words: member one time I needed a new winter coat and we went to Creighton's,

speaker: CS
words: my mother and I, and looked for a coat for me. I think it was prob

speaker: CS
words: ably the first ready made coat I'd had, because it was red and it had

speaker: CS
words: a red velvet collar, and I thought it was sooo beautiful. But, my

speaker: CS
words: mother really it was more than she could afford to pay, but

speaker: CS
words: Mr. Creighton happened to come back to where the coat was

speaker: CS
words: being shown, I think he knocked off a dollar or so, and my mother pur

speaker: CS
words: chased the coat for me, 'cause Mr. Creighton could see how badly I

speaker: CS
words: wanted that pretty red coat.

speaker: LS
words: You wouldn't shop around in those days?

speaker: CS
words: Well, no, I tell you, we didn't shop around too much, they knew you

speaker: CS
words: and like at Creighton's store, I don't know, I think our bill was paid

speaker: CS
words: twice a year, that was the way they did business. They didn't bill

speaker: CS
words: you monthly, as I remember. During the sales my mother would go in

speaker: CS
words: and stock up on the number of sheets and tablecloths and so on, she

speaker: CS
words: needed. Mr. Creighton went to Ireland every year and bought linen,

speaker: CS
words: so even in the early days you could buy very beautiful linens, here

speaker: CS
words: in Moscow. Like many homes, I suppose, our home we ate on a white

speaker: CS
words: tablecloth three times a day, and my father wouldn't have it other

speaker: CS
words: wise, and we all had white napkins. We never ate in the kitchen dur

speaker: CS
words: ing my time. Sometimes for Sunday night supper, I just thought it

speaker: CS
words: was so much fun, when I was a child, if we would have our supper in

speaker: CS
words: the kitchen and open the oven door, and we were all sitting close to

speaker: CS
words: gether, and it was such fun.

speaker: LS
words: In the book -

speaker: CS
words: All

speaker: LS
words: She mentions about how there were grades of groceries, grocery stores-

speaker: CS
words: Well, there were, really. That's true. There was a store down on

speaker: CS
words: Main Street, near my father's place of business, and

speaker: CS
words: he would get in fruit and watermelons and thingsr-- they didn t come

speaker: CS
words: in trucks, they came in hay wagons from Julietta and Lewiston and

speaker: CS
words: so on, and when he'd get in a load, why, everybody in Moscow would

speaker: CS
words: go down and get their fruit to can— it would be stacked in boxes

speaker: CS
words: out on the sidewalk and his prices were always supposed to be

speaker: CS
words: a little cheaper, but his family lived above the grocery store and

speaker: CS
words: I think he did most of the work himself, so his overhead probably was

speaker: CS
words: lower, and he could sell cheaper. I don't like to say anything more

speaker: CS
words: about. But, then, there were other stores in town: Mr. Swan had

speaker: CS
words: a very nice store, grocery store, and I think it was considered very

speaker: CS
words: first class. Probably his line of canned goods, and so on, was a lit

speaker: CS
words: tle more, but of very fine quality. And, then, as the years would go

speaker: CS
words: by, someone would start a grocery store and it would go a few years

speaker: CS
words: and they'd decide to close out or somebody would buy them out. So,

speaker: CS
words: over the years, there^quite a few grocery stores, as I remember.

speaker: LS
words: Would many of them sell meat?

speaker: CS
words: Well, no, we had meat markets. I think we had at least— I remember

speaker: CS
words: three meat markets when I was a little girl. And, that was one thing

speaker: CS
words: my father always purchased. He always bought the meat. And it would

speaker: CS
words: be delivered to our home. He'd stop in as he went to work and choose

speaker: CS
words: the meat that my mother had wanted. And, quite often, I know, we had

speaker: CS
words: round steak, and all the family liked that. He'd send home a great

speaker: CS
words: big round steak, and I think, perhaps, it was only twenty-five or

speaker: CS
words: thirty five cents. When it was prepared they would pound it with a

speaker: CS
words: knife, you know, and then dip it in flour and fry it. It may not have

speaker: CS
words: been so good for us, but it tasted awfully good. And then the drip

speaker: CS
words: pings made delicious gravy, you know, over potatoes or biscuits, and

speaker: CS
words: so on.

speaker: CS
words: At one time in Moscow, we were supposed to have had as many saloons

speaker: CS
words: as we did churches, that was one thing we talked about. And I think

speaker: CS
words: there were thirteen saloons and thirteen churches. And to go from

speaker: CS
words: our home, we always walked down Third Street, and either side of the

speaker: CS
words: street we went on, we had to pass a saloon, and, of course, the men

speaker: CS
words: were standing around outside and they would chew tobacco and spit on

speaker: CS
words: the sidewalk. I remember the ladies with their sweeping skirts, in

speaker: CS
words: those days, well, you held up your skirt when you passed those places,

speaker: CS
words: because— so it wouldn't touch the dirty sidewalk. And, of course,

speaker: CS
words: then we had no paving, and then every block was a crosswalk to get on

speaker: CS
words: the other side of the street. Well, those crosswalks were quite high,

speaker: CS
words: I'd judge eight or ten inches, because the mud would get so deep, you

speaker: CS
words: see, in the winter, fall and spring when it would rain. So they had

speaker: CS
words: to be high in order that you wouldn't sink clear in and lose your boots!!

speaker: LS
words: Those were all wooden planks.

speaker: CS
words: Wooden planks. And the sidewalks were of wood. But, I don't remem

speaker: CS
words: ber when the paving came, but, of course, the main streets were paved

speaker: CS
words: first and cement sidewalks were put down. 'Course in the residential

speaker: CS
words: neighborhoods we had board walks, sidewalks, we called 'em, long after

speaker: CS
words: the downtown streets had paving and cement sidewalks.

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember what the feeling was at the time about the saloons?

speaker: LS
words: About drinking?

speaker: CS
words: Well, I think it was just an accepted thing for a man to drink. My

speaker: CS
words: father was never a drinker, but I think, many, what we considered -Ive better

speaker: CS
words: class citizens, drank. But, my mother belonged to the WCTU,

speaker: CS
words: and she was a very ardent worker, and I can well remember when they

speaker: CS
words: would take petitions around to try to get.saloons out. At one time,

speaker: CS
words: the Prohibition Party always was on the county ticket, that I can

speaker: CS
words: remember. My father, we were, Republicans, always, but my

speaker: CS
words: mother, one time, they persuaded her to run on the Prohibition Party

speaker: CS
words: as state treasurer, but,of course, she didn't win. She took a great

speaker: CS
words: deal of teasing from my father over her experience in politi

speaker: LS
words: Did she actually run a campaign?

speaker: LS
words: Oh, no. Her name was just on the ticket. But, then, of course, when

speaker: LS
words: prohibition became the law, why, the saloons went out.

speaker: LS
words: What were some of the things you know that the WCTU did in the early

speaker: LS
words: days?

speaker: LS
words: Well, I think that was just- the most of it was just carry petitions

speaker: LS
words: around. They had their meetings. I never belonged to this, but I

speaker: LS
words: know my sister, one of my sisters, would never, and she and I are the

speaker: LS
words: only ones left- she doesn't even like to take a drink of wine, be

speaker: LS
words: cause she signed the pledge when she was a little girl— they had an

speaker: LS
words: organization for the children. But during my time, that never

speaker: LS
words: seemed to be- they didnit have that organization. But she said"l

speaker: LS
words: signed that pledge, and Idon't feel right yet to even take aglass

speaker: LS
words: of wine. Iwasn tin it, so Idon'tAknow what it was. But, Iimagine

speaker: LS
words: they had children's meetings and talked to them about the evi

speaker: LS
words: alcohol. Course, in our ' there was always a chapter

speaker: LS
words: on drinking alcohol, and the harm it did to your body. I don't know

speaker: LS
words: that they have aphysiology- Idon't think they teach physiology that

speaker: LS
words: way today, out of a book for children. It's part of their science.

speaker: LS
words: But we had a book, physiology book, and there was always a chapter on

speaker: LS
words: the harms of alcohol to the human body. And, I think we had very good, conscientious

speaker: LS
words: teachers. Some of them would be quite

speaker: LS
words: young when they started to teach, but they were very anxious for Mos

speaker: LS
words: cow children to learn. In some ways, I believe our learning in grade

speaker: LS
words: was superior in some ways than what children get now. Although,

speaker: LS
words: children know more than we did, because of their advantages

speaker: LS
words: of seeing and hearing and reading other boks, and so on. They have

speaker: LS
words: more knowledge, but I don't think they actually learned the funda

speaker: LS
words: menta

speaker: LS
words: as well as we did, of education.

speaker: LS
words: Is there anything in particular that they've changed?

speaker: CS
words: Well, I don't know too much about the education now, but, I do know

speaker: CS
words: that my grandchildren, with the new math, it takes them a great deal

speaker: CS
words: longer to figure out something than either their grandfather or their

speaker: CS
words: grandmother if it's a matter of dollar and cents, or how many, or

speaker: CS
words: something. Their way of going about it takes so much longer. 'Course,

speaker: CS
words: we're mature and we can see in a flash what it is, you know.

speaker: LS
words: I want to ask you a couple of questions about stores, too.

speaker: CS
words: Yes.

speaker: LS
words: Now, wasn't there ice cream parlors?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, indeed there was. In fact, at one time, I think that there were

speaker: CS
words: more than two, but it was a great pleasure to go to the ice cream par

speaker: CS
words: lor— and one of them had a table especially for children— like a

speaker: CS
words: wrought iron, or an iron table, you know, like porch furniture today,

speaker: CS
words: with little chairs, and, of course, it was lovely to go in there and

speaker: CS
words: sit at that little table. And, we bought sundaes more than anything,

speaker: CS
words: you know. We'd have ice cream with pineapple or strawberry or some

speaker: CS
words: thing over it, and that was quite the thing.

speaker: LS
words: Did they make their own ice cream?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, I think they did and it was very good ice cream, too.

speaker: LS
words: Did they have a lot of flavors?

speaker: CS
words: Well, not too many flavors. It was mostly just vanilla and chocolate,

speaker: CS
words: I think, maybe strawberry some of the times. Then you could have it

speaker: CS
words: made into a sundae. But I remember the one time befote my husband

speaker: CS
words: and I were married, we went into one of the confectionery stores,

speaker: CS
words: and ice cream parlors, that's the way they usually called them, be

speaker: CS
words: cause they made their candy too, much like Mrs. Hunter makes today

speaker: CS
words: their candies, you know. And we used to, in the winter, we d go in

speaker: CS
words: there and get oyster cocktail , oh, we just thought that was wonder

speaker: CS
words: ful. It was cold, you know, and why we'd like it in the winter I

speaker: CS
words: don't know, but we did.

speaker: LS
words: What was it?

speaker: CS
words: Well, it was oysters, raw oysters, with a spicy sauce over it. I

speaker: CS
words: supposCketchup was the foundation of the sauce. But, we thought that

speaker: CS
words: was fun.

speaker: CS
words: In our high school days we had lots of sleigh rides, because our

speaker: CS
words: snow seemed to stay so much longer than it does now. And, that was

speaker: CS
words: quite a popular form of entertainment in the winter to go on a sleigh

speaker: CS
words: ride party. hire either the livery stable or some farmer, and

speaker: CS
words: he'd put a hayrack on runners, and put straw in, and we'd all take

speaker: CS
words: our blankets, Sometimes we would go to Pullman and have oyster

speaker: CS
words: stew at some eating place over there. 'Course, that would take the

speaker: CS
words: whole evening to go over there and back, and that was quite an enter

speaker: CS
words: tainment, we thought that was a lot of fun. Or we'd go to somebody

speaker: CS
words: who lived in the country, and sometimes each of us took along some

speaker: CS
words: refreshments like a cake or something, and quite often they'd have

speaker: CS
words: hot cocoa for us. But, I remember one time, when I was in high

speaker: CS
words: school, Moscow was playing Genesee High School, and Mr.  took

speaker: CS
words: a group of us over, we started out right after school. We drove

speaker: CS
words: over the mountain road because, he went over that wav, it was shorter

speaker: CS
words: I think in order to get us there in time for the game. And we all

speaker: CS
words: were supposed to take along a sandwich to eat, because we would 'just

speaker: CS
words: have to go to the game as we took our sand-

speaker: CS
words: wich in a bag and ate it on the way over.

speaker: CS
words: And, the game was played

speaker: CS
words: and, I don't know whether it was in a warehouse, but they didn't have

speaker: CS
words: a gym or anything, you know, and while we were at the game a Chinook

speaker: CS
words: wind came up, o you know what they are? A warm wind,tfnd the snow

speaker: CS
words: all disappeared, and so we started home, and Mr. then came—

speaker: CS
words: the highway isn't as it is now, but there ' a more level road— and

speaker: CS
words: he came that way. from four or five miles out from Moscow we all

speaker: CS
words: had to get out and walk, because those horses couldn't pull — those

speaker: CS
words: runners wouldn't go with that many— twenty or twenty-five young people

speaker: CS
words: in the wagon. Of course, it took most of the night, walking so far

speaker: CS
words: because I know my mother wasn't concerned about me because I was going

speaker: CS
words: home to stay all night with one of my friends, io when I came ia walk

speaker: CS
words: ing in about seven o'clock in the morning, she wondered why I came

speaker: CS
words: home so early, and I told her we were just getting home from Genesee.

speaker: CS
words: But we had a bad misfortune out where the railroad tracks cross down

speaker: CS
words: South Main, you know, fhe runner on the sled got caught in the track

speaker: CS
words: and Mr.  was driving the sled and we were all trailing behind,

speaker: CS
words: of course, but we were pretty weary by that time, and the sled being

speaker: CS
words: caught in the track, it threw him out and he hit his head and it knock

speaker: CS
words: ed him out. Well, we were all very frightened I don't think he was

speaker: CS
words: knocked out for long, but it was fortunate that he was so near his

speaker: CS
words: livery barn. And I don't even remember who won the game, (chuckles)

speaker: CS
words: after the experience of that.

speaker: LS
words: What kind of game was it?

speaker: CS
words: Basketball. They were playing basketball then. And we were in that

speaker: CS
words: old, old high school, you know, that I told you about on Third Street.

speaker: CS
words: And there was no place there, of course, so they rented a warehouse

speaker: CS
words: down on Main Street, where one of the grain companies is now,

speaker: CS
words: it was just a big wooden building, you know, and that's where they

speaker: CS
words: played basketball. I used to play girls basketball in that place.

speaker: LS
words: basketball very popular then?

speaker: CS
words: Well, it was then, and then it went out and now, of course, it's back

speaker: CS
words: again. They had us play six girls to a team then. The rules were a

speaker: CS
words: little different than boys, but we wanted to play boys rules, but we

speaker: CS
words: weren't permitted to.

speaker: LS
words: Would you play around with different school?

speaker: CS
words: i never was on the team, I wasn't good enough, but they did play the

speaker: CS
words: other teams around. Mrs.  was the center because she was so tall,

speaker: CS
words: she was Mabel Drury. They did play different towns around. I don't

speaker: CS
words: think there were too many outside games played, and there weren't too

speaker: CS
words: many girls involved in the basketball program.

speaker: LS
words: Something you said?

speaker: LS
words: Our parties— sometimes, I know when I was a senior in high school

speaker: LS
words: we used to have parties there in the old school house, but there was

speaker: LS
words: no recreation room or anything, we just had to play Skip-to-my-Lou,

speaker: LS
words: and so on in the halls. They were that sort of parties. But the

speaker: LS
words: school would let us use the building. Of course, we had to always

speaker: LS
words: ask some teacher to chap«rone us. But, the year I was a senior in

speaker: LS
words: high school, there wasn't room for us to have a study hall, so we

speaker: LS
words: just had to kind of stick around in the halls and keep our books on

speaker: LS
words: the window sills someplace, you know. It was great fun (chuckles)

speaker: LS
words: but we didn't get any studying done in school time, we had to do our

speaker: LS
words: studying at home, because we had no place to go, although we did hang

speaker: LS
words: out down in the school superintendent's office, and how he ever stood

speaker: LS
words: twenty— there were twenty in my class— all of us in there, I'll never

speaker: LS
words: know, but he was a very good natured man. The new building, which is

speaker: LS
words: the office building on Third Street now, was being built then, and he

speaker: LS
words: was so, Mr. Randall, was so wrapped up in that new building. I don't

speaker: LS
words: think that he actually knew that we were he was over to the

speaker: LS
words: new building so much— that he actually knew that we were just making

speaker: LS
words: use of his office like we did. So, we sort of had open campus then,

speaker: LS
words: (chuckles) the senior class.

speaker: LS
words: What were some of the social events? Do you remember?

speaker: CS
words: Well, Fourth of July, they made a great deal of Fourth of July here

speaker: CS
words: with a big parade, and the stores would have floats, you know, and

speaker: CS
words: the band would play, and then, usually, they would have several dance

speaker: CS
words: halls where the bands would play during the afternoon and evening. I

speaker: CS
words: remember one Fourth of July, they had draped all the store fronts with

speaker: CS
words: bunting, red, white and blue bunting, and it rained just terrifically.

speaker: CS
words: Well, the bunting would drip down on all the white dresses, you know?

speaker: CS
words: But the dance halls were very popular that year, because people, even

speaker: CS
words: if they weren't dancing ' crowd in, so the floor space was very

speaker: CS
words: sparce, because there were so many people standing around the edges

speaker: CS
words: to get out of the rain. We had our special friends that we had par

speaker: CS
words: ties in our homes. I remember, I belonged to a club— and I don't

speaker: CS
words: know, there must have been twenty or twenty-five of us boys and girls

speaker: CS
words: and we called it the QAE Club, and that meant quit at eleven!! We

speaker: CS
words: had to go home at eleven, that was the rule., And, we were always say

speaker: CS
words: ing, we wished our mothers would let us change it to the QAT Club,

speaker: CS
words: twelve or two, then we could stay 'til twelve, you see. We

speaker: CS
words: were grade schoolers, what would be junior high, and those parties

speaker: CS
words: were in our homes. I remember I had a Halloween party one time, and

speaker: CS
words: it made a house full, but, of course, everybody came^masquarade and

speaker: CS
words: we had great fun pretending we didn't know who people were, and so on,

speaker: CS
words: and ducking for apples, I guess you called it, and bittg ^a dough nut

speaker: CS
words: tied to a string, and all the Halloween stunts we used to do.

speaker: LS
words: Were there many tricks played on Halloween?

speaker: CS
words: Well, of course, there were privies in those days. And the bigger

speaker: CS
words: boys would tip over the privies, or steal the gates — the front gate

speaker: CS
words: off of the fence. There was always quite a bit of that, so it was

speaker: CS
words: pretty wise to put your gate away for the evening, but the privy—

speaker: CS
words: there wasn't much you could do about that!! (chuckles) But, our

speaker: CS
words: next door neighbor was always so concerned— my dad never seemed

speaker: CS
words: to be too concerned, but he sort of patrolled his property the whole

speaker: CS
words: night, I think. And, of course, the kids, knowing that, would always

speaker: CS
words: do something to him. I don't remember of being mixed up in anything

speaker: CS
words: very much. We didn't trick or treat, we just didn't know about that

speaker: CS
words: in Moscow then. But, we'd make tick-tacks. We'd notch a spool, and

speaker: CS
words: my brother usually made mine, and put it on a little stick or pencil

speaker: CS
words: then wrap a string around it and go up to someone's window and pull

speaker: CS
words: the string, and, of course, the spool would turn and make quite a

speaker: CS
words: racket. Or, we'd ring somebody's doorbell and run, that was the ex

speaker: CS
words: tent of our mischief, I think. But, we just didn't know about knock

speaker: CS
words: ing on a door and saying, "Trick or treat." I don't know why we went.

speaker: CS
words: If somebody didn't have a party, we'd go out with out jack-o-lanterns

speaker: CS
words: around our own neighborhoods, of course, but we didn't expect them to

speaker: CS
words: give us anything at all.

speaker: CS
words: I think one of the nicest childhood parties I remember was the one

speaker: CS
words: that Carol Rttrie's mother gave her, and she shipped in the ice cream

speaker: CS
words: that came on the noon train from Spokane, as I told you, it had all

speaker: CS
words: been molded like little animals rabbits or chickens or so on, and

speaker: CS
words: we thought that was the most elegant party, because we had never seen

speaker: CS
words: ice cream molded like that before.

speaker: LS
words: Was that QAE Club, was that at all formal?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, no, no.

speaker: LS
words: Anyone could come?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, no, we had a membership. Yes, it was a membership club. And

speaker: CS
words: I think there were about twenty to twenty-five of us, and we never

speaker: CS
words: went home with a boy, or anything, we'd just all go in groups, you

speaker: CS
words: know. And, the fellow that lived the farth est, I suppose, he had

speaker: CS
words: to tackle the last part of the way by himself.

speaker: LS
words: Did you dance?

speaker: CS
words: Well, we did. We didn't go to dances like children do now, even in

speaker: CS
words: high school. But, if we had older brothers and sisters we'd just

speaker: CS
words: gradually learn how to dance at home, you know. I have a terrible

speaker: CS
words: story to tell, but I don't know whether you would want to tape it or

speaker: CS
words: not.

speaker: CS
words: It was— I don't believe we were — well, we must have been in

speaker: CS
words: high school— but we had a Leap Year Sunday School party. The girls

speaker: CS
words: class in the Presbyterian Sunday School and a boys' class, they were

speaker: CS
words: about the same size, so each girl asked a boy to go to this Leap Year

speaker: CS
words: party. Well, there was a woman teacher for the boys' class and we

speaker: CS
words: had a man teacher, the girls class, and so we went to — this girl

speaker: CS
words: was a niece living with her aunt that year, going to school— it was

speaker: CS
words: the Day home, we went there to the party. That was the big home of

speaker: CS
words: the city. So, we had sort of made a plan that after the party was

speaker: CS
words: over and the refreshments were done, we'd all start home, and then

speaker: CS
words: we d go back and dance. always bothered me, and I don't believe

speaker: CS
words: our nice Sunday School teacher ever knew what we had done, because,

speaker: CS
words: this girl, her aunt was away, and she'd asked the next door — a young

speaker: CS
words: lady and her fiancee to come in and chaparone— but our dancing was

speaker: CS
words: very hoppity, I imagine, (chuckles) But that always bothered me, be

speaker: CS
words: cause this Sunday School teacher, he was such a peach, and we'd have

speaker: CS
words: Sunday School parties, and he played the piano, and that was such a

speaker: CS
words: great thing to do in those days, to stand around the piano and sing.

speaker: CS
words: Well, he'd play all these popular songs, sheet music, you know, and

speaker: CS
words: we'd sing, and he was so good to play for us to sing, and then we

speaker: CS
words: treated him that way. always kind of bothered me. (chuckles)

speaker: CS
words: He was always one of my good friends, but I had a guilty feeling.

speaker: CS
words: Were there any circuses?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes, that was a big day when the circus came to town. And, of

speaker: CS
words: course, they traveled by train. And we'd have our alarm clock waken

speaker: CS
words: us, and several friends, we'd get together and go down to watch

speaker: CS
words: the train come in, and it usually came in around four o'clock in the

speaker: CS
words: morning, and it'd be cold, you know, but it was

speaker: CS
words: They would take off the elephants first, becausi

speaker: CS
words: big poles and the tents, the canvasses, to wherever the circus was to

speaker: CS
words: be. And we had several big open spaces, qijite close downtown in those

speaker: CS
words: days where the circus could be. And, so, after we'd watch the circus

speaker: CS
words: wagons, all painted, you know, beautifully painted. And, of course,

speaker: CS
words: very interesting,

speaker: CS
words: e they had to pull the animals inside, we could hear

speaker: CS
words: tigers, and so on. They weren't open, because we weren't supposed to

speaker: CS
words: see them until- Would hurry and go out to the circus grounds, because if they carried

speaker: CS
words: water to the elephants by pails and that's the way they had to do it,

speaker: CS
words: from hydrants, you know, nearby hydrants, why, they'd get a ticket to

speaker: CS
words: the circus. And, Harry did that, and I know my brother used to do

speaker: CS
words: that. And he 'd get a ticket for admission to the circus..

speaker: CS
words: But even,

speaker: CS
words: though we didn't have too much money, my father thought we should al

speaker: CS
words: ways go to the circus. He thought that was a very important thing for

speaker: CS
words: us to do. So there was always money for us to go to the circus. And

speaker: CS
words: we'd always go early, because, they had the animals: in one big tent

speaker: CS
words: the lions and the

speaker: CS
words: with the cages around the sides, and you could walk around and

speaker: CS
words: look at them before you went into the main tent to take your seat.

speaker: CS
words: And, of course, I don't remember of ever sitting in a reserved seat,

speaker: CS
words: which had a little back and was right at the center ring. These were

speaker: CS
words: usually three ring circuses. But, you'd try to get as near that as

speaker: CS
words: you could find a seat, you know, so you could see better. And

speaker: CS
words: then they opened the circus with a grand entry, you know, the parade.

speaker: CS
words: The bespangled girls riding on the elephants' heads, and the riders

speaker: CS
words: on the horses, and the cages coming around. But, in the morning, a

speaker: CS
words: thing which they never do any more, they'd have a parade on Main

speaker: CS
words: street, and that was one reason that. came to town so early, and

speaker: CS
words: of course, many children, that would be all they'd get to see

speaker: CS
words: ithe parade, because, the country people would drive in for the parade.

speaker: CS
words: I think it was usually scheduled for ten o'clock and it never came

speaker: CS
words: along til about eleven. And that was the one day of the year that

speaker: CS
words: didn't my father wouldn't.come home for the noon meal. So, my mother would,

speaker: CS
words: oh, I thought this was lovely, she'd buy buns and balogna when we'd

speaker: CS
words: go to town and we'd have cookies or something and we'd go to my fath

speaker: CS
words: er's place of business and eat. But every other working day of the

speaker: CS
words: year, he came home for his noon meal. But circus day, because of

speaker: CS
words: the parade, he didn't expect anyone to stay home to get him a meal.

speaker: CS
words: And, oh, that was very thrilling to have the parade go down Main

speaker: CS
words: Street.

speaker: LS
words: (very noisy) Was there only one circus?

speaker: CS
words: Yes,one. Oh, well,^sometimes we used to have-Ringling Circus

speaker: CS
words: first wasn't with Barnum and Bailey, you know. And we didn't get both

speaker: CS
words: big ones, every year, but then they— they were talking about it on

speaker: CS
words: "What's My Line?" the other night— I've forgotten the name of the

speaker: CS
words: smaller circus that used to come, and then we used to have dog and

speaker: CS
words: pony shows come. Well, they just had dog and pony performOnS.And

speaker: CS
words: they were delightful shows. They'd have a band, but oh, the ponies

speaker: CS
words: would perform, you know, and they had white ponies and little dark

speaker: CS
words: ponies and Shetland ponies. Horses. And, then, the little dogs, you

speaker: CS
words: know, would go through all their a , jumping through hoops. Course,

speaker: CS

speaker: CS
words: to see them now, because they really are a rather tranquil show for

speaker: CS
words: a child to see. They were delightful. One thing that the circuses

speaker: CS
words: the statues. Have you ever heard of that? Well, in one end of the

speaker: CS
words: tent they'd have this big thing all covered with white canvas or mus

words: lin, or something, and then at this time of the show the big lights

speaker: CS
words: and they'd pose as the Three Graces, or something, you know, and the

speaker: CS
words: band would play appropriate music, and then the cover would come down

speaker: CS
words: and they'd pose for another, and to me, that was just most exquisite.

speaker: LS
words: if.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I don't think they ever do that any more.

speaker: LS
words: Did they have acrobats?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. And, of course, they always had nets below 'em. But then

speaker: CS
words: the crowd'd always go, ohhhhhh! you know, for fear they wouldn't

speaker: CS
words: catch the— some  through the air, you know. I

speaker: CS
words: Never remember of seeing anyone hurt, at all, because they always

speaker: CS
words: used nets. r6ourse, I think some of the performers today, especially

speaker: CS
words: European circuses, don't use careful care— they're so sure of

speaker: CS
words: themselves, or they're so much better trained, perhaps.

speaker: LS
words: Did you ever see medicine shows?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, I remember one, that came to town, and it's right where Short's

speaker: CS
words: Chapel is. I don't know what the medicine was. But they had this,

speaker: CS
words: sort of like a minstrel show, you know, two or three actors would

speaker: CS
words: put it on, and then the salesman would go through the crowd

speaker: CS
words: the medicine. I don't remember that the Moddy family ever bought any,

speaker: CS
words: I can't remember that we did. And, I think that it was just mostly

speaker: CS
words: children ' went to the medicine show.

speaker: LS
words: They just put on a little skit?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, just a tiny little stage built

speaker: CS
words: And, then a hypnotist would come to town. And they'd always put

speaker: CS
words: somebody to sleep in their store window,

speaker: CS
words: I think at one time they got a big block of granite from my father

words: to go on this hyptnotized person's stomach.^ And then, perhaps, at

speaker: CS
words: a certain time they'd have a show, the hypnotist would in the Opera

speaker: CS
words: House or the GAR Hall, or wherever it was, and wake them from the stage

speaker: CS
words: you see. He'd have an accomplise there with him, but he'd awaken them

speaker: CS
words: from the stage and then he'd do all of his disappearing tracks, you

words: know— soom. Then, we used to have chautauquas here at one time.

words: Well, we did in Moscow, too. And, I I was of high school age

speaker: CS
words: then, and it lasted a week, and it was morning and afternoon. And,

words: oh, people would just flock to the chautauqua , you know. They'd put

speaker: CS
words: uf in our city park a tent, "There the chautauqua would be, and you

words: bought your ticket for the whole week. I don't think they sold tickets

speaker: CS
words: for each event,

speaker: CS

speaker: CS
words: They'd have lectures, you know, and then maybe a musical group. It

words: was all brought in talent.

speaker: LS
words: What would the lectures be on?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, I don?t even remember that.

speaker: CS
words: Well, no, I don't believe they were religious. Margaret J^iaoke

speaker: CS

words: about it, but it seems all the Protestant churches had gone in on

speaker: CS
words: this. And right back of the old post office, where the old Christian

words: Church used to be, it's a parking lot now, they built a wooden taber

words: nacle, they called it, just of boards, you know. And, she said in

words: had a childrens'— well, I think I was enough older that— I'm enough

words: older than Margaret, because I never went there to any children^ meet

words: ing. I don't even remember of going at all. But Margaret said she

words: and Marguerite jlin went every night after school, and Lillian Ottness

words: to this childrens' meeting. But I think that I was probably

words: Watkins, too, but it was no relation— he said, "I'll never pull another

speaker: CS
words: tooth for Mrs. Moody." And it scared my father to death, he thought

speaker: CS
words: she'd died or something, and he just meant he'd finished the job. But

speaker: CS
words: she was very uncomfortable I remember after that. And.they waited sev

speaker: CS
words: eral months before they put the new teeth in, you know, 'cause I know

speaker: CS
words: when I had to have my upper teeth extracted, they put the teeth in im

speaker: CS
words: mediately because they'd taken a mold already and made them.

speaker: LS
words: Can you tell me about your grandmother who lived out on—

speaker: CS
words: Well, yes, that was the grandmother, that was my mother's mother who

speaker: CS
words: lived in Salubria, where I said my mother came to. And she married

speaker: CS
words: Mr. Smith, we always called him, we never called him grandpa or any

speaker: CS
words: thing but Mr. Smith. And they bought a place down on Fix Ridge, and

speaker: CS
words: Grandma was just sort of the do-gooder on that ridge. Anybody was sick

speaker: CS
words: they'd call Grandma, 'cause she had all these old time remedies, you

speaker: CS
words: know. And anybody died}Grandma had to go and help lay them out, as

speaker: CS
words: she called it. But, they did have phones, but they had a signal that

speaker: CS
words: if anything happened central rang this one ring and everybody took

speaker: CS
words: their phones down, so they would know whatever had happened, you know.

speaker: CS
words: And that always seemed like that was quite neat to me, when I was a

speaker: CS
words: child. Mama went down several times during the good part of the year,

speaker: CS
words: the weather, and Iwas alittle girl and Iwent with her. But, we had

speaker: CS
words: to ford a little stream out here someplace beyond Lgnnville, and we'd

speaker: CS
words: start out early in the morning 'cause it took us pretty near all day to

speaker: CS
words: get there, because the horse never went at avery good gait for my

speaker: CS
words: mother, kinda walked all the way. An my mother always hoped that I'd

speaker: CS
words: be having anap when we came to the stream, because, as Iremember you

speaker: CS
words: could- it was just like that- but it scared me, anyway, that bumping

speaker: CS
words: across the rocks. And, I've never found the place where that was, but

speaker: CS
words: of course, it's so different now. Then I'd get out at the top of the

speaker: CS
words: hill above Grandma's house, they lived kind of down in alittle valley

speaker: CS
words: and go down through the orchard and beat Mama there, of course, 'cause

speaker: CS
words: she'd have to go around and go through the lane to get to the house.

speaker: CS
words: But, Grandma had feather beds and we didn't have feather beds, we just

speaker: CS
words: had mattresses, and Ohhh, Ithought that was just heaven to sleep in a

speaker: CS
words: feather bed.

speaker: LS
words: You'd stay over night?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes, we'd stay several days when we'd go like that, 'cause it was

speaker: CS
words: an all-day trip almost for my mother to get there, and we stayed several

speaker: CS
words: days. Iknow we often went when the peaches were ripe down there. And,

speaker: CS
words: Grandma— they had quite a prune orchard. They had quite a business

speaker: CS
words: of drying prunes, that was quite the thing here in the early days. Peo

speaker: CS
words: ple would grow prunes and dry 'em. And, I think they must have sold

speaker: CS
words: them to the stores. But they had a house dryer, where they had some

speaker: CS
words: kind of fire, I don't know what it was, and the prunes would be put in

speaker: CS
words: on chicken wire racks and they'd dry them. I don't know why they

speaker: CS
words: didn't taste smokey, but they didn't, they were delicious. 'Cause we

speaker: CS
words: always had a sack hanging in the buttry, when I was a child, and we'd

speaker: CS
words: play sort of 'cops and robbers', you know, and the buttry was always

speaker: CS
words: the jail, but there was kind of an advantage to being in jail, because

speaker: CS
words: you could eat dried prunes and dried apples, (chuckles)

speaker: LS
words: Did your mother dry the apples, too?

speaker: CS
words: Yes. I remember she did. They'd put sheets up on the wood house roof

speaker: CS
words: and we'd spread just think of peeling all those apples— and then

speaker: CS
words: my brother, probably, was sent up to spread them out and then they put

speaker: CS
words: a net- what is this window netting, what did they call that?

speaker: LS
words: Mosquito?

speaker: CS
words: Mosquito net over them, to keep the bugs off. But they never thought

speaker: CS
words: about the dust, because our street wasn't paved and the dust would get

speaker: CS
words: about that in the summer. And a vehicle happened to go by the

speaker: CS
words: dust would roll in. But we always sprinkled down our road— the street

speaker: CS
words: in front of our house; ^several of the neighbors did, but on beyond us

speaker: CS
words: they never sprinkled, so we always managed to get quite a bit of dust.

speaker: CS
words: That was one job I had to do, was sweeping off the veranda every day,

speaker: CS
words: and it was quite a job, all that dust. But people then used to sit out

speaker: CS
words: on their porch in the evenings, you know, it was hot, but you sat out

speaker: CS
words: there and the neighbors would come over and visit. My father always

speaker: CS
words: sat out and read his paper in the evening until it got too dark to

speaker: CS
words: read. But, you used your porches a great deal in those days. And, so,

speaker: CS
words: that was one of my jobs, was to sweep off the veranda every morning.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have other jobs?

speaker: CS
words: Well, filling the woodbox, and then sprinkling the lawn.

speaker: LS
words: How would you do that?

speaker: CS
words: Well, we had a sprinkler, but, to get the edges I would hold the hose

speaker: CS
words: and sprinkle. And I know if I was anxious to go someplace, if I'd make

speaker: CS
words: it look awfully wet on the sidewalks and everything, my father'd think

speaker: CS
words: I'd done a pretty good job of sprinkling, so I could get going, but,

speaker: CS
words: actually it was just all surface. But, I was really supposed to start

speaker: CS
words: the hose late in the afternoon and give the lawn a good watering.

speaker: LS
words: Did you have much of a garden?

speaker: LS
words: Oh, yes. We had these two lots, you see, south of our house, and there

speaker: LS
words: were trees, and then in the center was all open with a garden spot. We

speaker: LS
words: always had our own potatoes and peas and beans and things, and corn,

speaker: LS
words: and my father used to hire the next door neighbor to come over and spade

speaker: LS
words: it up for him and then he'd plant it. Every evening he'd go out and hoe

speaker: LS
words: and work In the garden. And it was always a delight to go down in

speaker: LS
words: fruit cellar in the fall after everything was harvested. There were

speaker: LS
words: sacks and sacks of potatoes and all the shelves full of can

speaker: LS
words: ned fruit, because the stores— you just could only buy oranges and ban

speaker: LS
words: anas in the winter, you know, that was all you could ever buy. We didn't

speaker: LS
words: even have head lettuce or anything like that in the winter, then. But

speaker: LS
words: we had our own beets and carrots and they were stored and parsnips and

speaker: LS
words: all the things that you could grow that would keep, you know. But, I

speaker: LS
words: don't remember, we never grew our own tomatoes in those days. We always

speaker: LS
words: had to buy them. But several gardeners around town would— out in the

speaker: LS
words: country grew tomatoes. But, I don't know why we never thought we could

speaker: LS
words: have tomatoes in our garden. We thought they wouldn't ripen, you know

speaker: LS
words: the frost came. But, I think the varieties are better now and

speaker: LS
words: are earlier and so on, and so we can ripen tomatoes easily in Moscow.

speaker: LS
words: Did you can any meat or anything like that?

speaker: CS
words: No. We never did. We never did that, I know lots of people did. What

speaker: CS
words: we did do: in the spring of the year, my grandmother would always give

speaker: CS
words: us two little pigs. Our lot was big so we had a pigpen, you know, and

speaker: CS
words: we had our own chickens. But then, in the early winter, later than this

speaker: CS
words: when it started to get real cold, my father'd have a man come and butcher,

speaker: CS
words: but they never smoked the meat. It was packed down in barrels with salt, and so that

speaker: CS
words: made it- always had to be parboiled, you

speaker: CS
words: know, it'd be salty. But it was good. But I don't know what about the

speaker: CS
words: hams and things, they made sausage, but I don't remember whether they

speaker: CS
words: to be used fresh, or how they used them, cause they

speaker: CS
words: weren't smoked. Unless this man took them and smoked them. He must have

speaker: CS
words: because we'd have bacon. So this man who did the butchering must haYe

speaker: CS
words: smoked the meat at his home.

speaker: LS
words: Was it unusual for a family in the city to have a big garden? You know,

speaker: LS
words: chickens—

speaker: CS
words: Well, not in our part of town, it wasn't unusual. But down near Main

speaker: CS
words: Street people didn't usually have that sort of thing. My friends, I

speaker: CS
words: don't think that lived near in— one's father was a doctor and the

speaker: CS
words: other^was a blacksmith, and they never did have at their homes, because

speaker: CS
words: the blacksmith because the girl who had the blacksmith as a father lived

speaker: CS
words: where Safeway is now, their home was there, and his shop was right

speaker: CS
words: where the theatre is, the movie theatre. And then Dr. had a

speaker: CS
words: later he had a hospital over where the apartments are on First Street,

speaker: CS
words: across from the United Church down there. But, their home was up on

speaker: CS
words: First Street and they had no garden. They had a barn, they had a horse,

speaker: CS
words: but they had no garden. So, I don't believe as a rule, people who lived

speaker: CS
words: closer in— we lived then what was considered quite far out, and then

speaker: CS
words: having that big a lot, why, we always had a garden. And it was probably

speaker: CS
words: a necessity with having six children. And, then, Mr. Ramstad,

speaker: CS
words: who lived near us, it's Allen Ramstfad, who's in Creightons, his grand

speaker: CS
words: father, they lived right across the alley back of us, and he had a

speaker: CS
words: great big orchard, and we bought cherries, I know. He'd bring over

speaker: CS
words: great milk pails of cherries and my mother would can those, 'cause

speaker: CS
words: we had cherry pie, and apple pie, and so on. Lots of cherry pie and

speaker: CS
words: mincemeat pie and so on, in the winter. But, I know, Mrs. wn0

speaker: CS
words: was Aunt Elsie in Carol's book, Aunt Elsie who brought Carol up, used

speaker: CS
words: to say my oldest sister never could go anyplace on Saturday, because

speaker: CS
words: she had to make cherry pies. And she said she made seven. Well, I

speaker: CS
words: can't even imagine one family making seven cherry pies at a time, but

speaker: CS
words: Miss Elsie said that was the way it was at the Moody house, so I don't

speaker: CS
words: know. I don't remember that.

speaker: LS
words: Did you ever eat out?

speaker: CS
words: Well, no, no, we didn't. Actually the hotels were the only places who

speaker: CS
words: had diningrooms. I don't remember of being taken to the hotel to eat,

speaker: CS
words: ever, when I was a child. If you went to another town and stayed in

speaker: CS
words: the hotel, then you did. If you went to Spokane, or anything, you know.

speaker: CS
words: But, being in Moscow, I don't remember of ever eating out at the hotel,

speaker: CS
words: as a little child. 'Course, you went to church suppers, and as I told

speaker: CS
words: you before, the friends always took me to the bean bake and the GAR

speaker: CS
words: supper, and so on. So, those were the way you ate out.

speaker: LS
words: bean bake?

speaker: CS
words: Well, the GAR is the Grand Army of the Republic, the Civil War veterans

speaker: CS
words: used to have that, and these friends, the father belonged, so they al

speaker: CS
words: ways took me along with them.

speaker: LS
words: You said, when you were talking about that, that those were the old

speaker: LS
words: timers?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh, they were. We thought that they were, because the men all had

speaker: CS
words: great white beards, and so on, and we thought that they were— well,

speaker: CS
words: they were really the people who settled around here first, those people.

speaker: LS
words: Do you remember any of the older people that impressed you?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, -

speaker: LS
words: You know, the early settlers?

speaker: CS
words: Well, Mr. Neil^was one, and, of course, his daughter was my friend,

speaker: CS
words: but I thought he was pretty fine. And then, they had a friend, and I

speaker: CS
words: think he was an old bachelor, Mr. Mc Bane, who had this great, white

speaker: CS
words: beard, and I always was a little in awe of him, you know, with this

speaker: CS
words: great, white beard. 'Course now, Harvey Smith's father, Mr. H. R. Smith,

speaker: CS
words: was always superintendent of our Sunday School, and I think he had a

speaker: CS
words: smaller beard, and I think we all stood in a little awe of him. But,

speaker: CS
words: much after I was a little child, he was superintendent so many years—

speaker: CS
words: some little child went home and told their mother that they'd seen God

speaker: CS
words: that day at Sunday School. And they called Mr. H. R. Smith God, that's

speaker: CS
words: Harvey's father, whom you're going to talk to tomorrow. He was a law

speaker: CS
words: yer.

speaker: CS
words: But in those days, the University wasn't so big and the town wasn't

speaker: CS
words: so big, and there was much sociability and good feeling between— we

speaker: CS
words: always went to things on the campus, even when I was a small child.

speaker: CS
words: 'Course, the things were recitals and declamatory contests and that

speaker: CS
words: sort of thing, debates, you know. But, the townspeople always went to

speaker: CS
words: those things. That was the thing that we all did. And, I think that

speaker: CS
words: was a good feeling. And the women of the town; the women who lived on

speaker: CS
words: that side, on University side, would have their afternoon at home one

speaker: CS
words: day a week and the women who lived on this side would stay at home for

speaker: CS
words: callers, you see, and they'd have their little teatables and serve tea.

speaker: LS
words: Who would come by?

speaker: CS
words: Well, the women from— who had friends on this side would come and

speaker: CS
words: call. They did a great deal of calling in those days. And the first

speaker: CS
words: time you went to call on anyone, you always left your card, you see.

speaker: CS
words: They always had a little tray or plate near the door you'd always

speaker: CS
words: leave your card. 'Course, even after I was married people called on me

speaker: CS
words: and left their cards.

speaker: LS
words: Women would have cards?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh, with their names on. Mrs. So-and-so. But that was rather a

speaker: CS
words: nice custom. We don't take time to call like that much anymore. And

speaker: CS
words: it was a nice custom. Of course, our groups of friends are so much

speaker: CS
words: larger that you just don't do that sort of thing. But it's too bad.

speaker: CS
words: Especially for newcomers who come to town. They have to sort of make

speaker: CS
words: their own means of getting acquainted, you know, it seems, by going to

speaker: CS
words: church or perhaps.asked to join some club or something. But, I think

speaker: CS
words: that was a very good idea. 'Course, when I was a little girl, we thought

speaker: CS
words: it was pretty nice when we were asked to serve punch at the University

speaker: CS
words: dances. They'd fix up a real— like the Junior Prom, or something

speaker: CS
words: beautiful table and ask several little town girls to come up to serve

speaker: CS
words: the punch. And we thought that was something, to be asked to

speaker: CS
words: come to the University to serve punch at a dance.

speaker: LS
words: What would the dance be like? Would they get real dressed up?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes!! They really dressed up, the girLls, had evening gowns. And

speaker: CS
words: I don't think they wore— the men wore Tuxedos or full dress or anything,

speaker: CS
words: but they had suits— the men wore suits. But they— we had cabs- horse

speaker: CS
words: cabs in those days, and so the young man, if he could afford it, he'd

speaker: CS
words: take his girl, especially if she lived across town, to the dance in a

speaker: CS
words: cab. But they always picked the little girls up, that was part of the

speaker: CS
words: expense of the class that was giving the dance. to get the little

speaker: CS
words: girls there that served, and we thought that. Only I was

speaker: CS
words: always the last one home, 'cause we lived the fartherest away, and

speaker: CS
words: my mother didn't approve or appreciate that very much. But it- I

speaker: CS
words: think those of us who had older brothers and sisters in college, we

speaker: CS
words: really had two educations, because we knew everything that was going

speaker: CS
words: on when we were children, you know, they'd come home and talk about,

speaker: CS
words: and then when we got older ourselves and did the same sort of thing,

speaker: CS
words: why, we sort of lived it before, don't you know.

speaker: CS
words: One time, maybe I told you this before— We were over to a

speaker: CS
words: party, it was about this time of the year, to a little friend's house

speaker: CS
words: and it got dark early, and it was just pouring rain, and my two little

speaker: CS
words: friends and I were coming home,'cause we lived on this side, and this

speaker: CS
words: big, tall man came along and asked us to walk under his umbrella, he

speaker: CS
words: always carried a great, big umbrella, he was from Canada, rather Eng

speaker: CS
words: lish, and we didn't know who the man was, but, oh, he asked us so many

speaker: CS
words: questions, what our names were, and if we had brothers and sisters in

speaker: CS
words: college, and so on. Afterwards, we found out it was the new president

speaker: CS
words: of the University, Doctor Mc  and there we d told the whole

speaker: CS
words: life history of our brothers and sisters who were in college, and they

speaker: CS
words: were pretty put out with us. Because the school wasn't so big then,

speaker: CS
words: and, of course, he knew who they were when he heard their names. I

speaker: CS
words: expect he wondered why three little girls were out around five o'clock

speaker: CS
words: on such a day. He ate his dinners down at the hotel, and he

speaker: CS
words: was going for his dinner.
